title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Quiz 12
text:
These practice problems cover Lecture 31 (study design and inference language: random sample vs convenience sample; randomized experiment vs observational study; causation, confounding, and chance), Lecture 32 (one-way ANOVA concepts and interpreting overall F-test output), and Lecture 33 (chi-square test of independence concepts and interpreting overall test output).
This quiz is concepts only: no hand-calculation is required.





Work without notes first, then check your work.
For multiple-choice items, choose the best single answer.
Unless a question says otherwise, interpret significance at alpha = 0.05.





For each scenario, classify whether observations are paired/dependent or independent/not paired.


Thirty students have their resting heart rate measured before and after a 6-week exercise program. Their measurements before and after are:

Paired/dependent by student

Independent/not paired within students





Two separate sections of STAT 109 take different versions of a quiz. Each student appears in only one section. The quiz scores are:

Paired/dependent between the sections

Independent/not paired between the sections





Researchers match each smoker with one nonsmoker of similar age and sex, then compare lung function between matched pairs. The measurements of lung function are:

Paired/dependent between the groups of smokers and non-smokers

Independent/not paired between the groups of smokers and non-smokers





Plant height is measured for seedlings randomly assigned to fertilizer A, B, or C, with each seedling receiving only one fertilizer. The plant height data is:

Paired/dependent between the fertilizer types

Independent/not paired between the fertilizer types








Suppose boxplots are used to compare blood-pressure reduction across three treatments. The medians are visibly different, and one group is shifted noticeably higher than the other two.
Which statement is best?

The boxplot is descriptive evidence of at least one group mean difference.

The boxplot proves all population means are different.

The boxplot shows no evidence of any differences.





You fit fit <- lm(y ~ group, data = dat) and run anova(fit). The output shows a p-value = 0.012.
At alpha = 0.05, which interpretation is best?

Reject H0; the data provide evidence that at least one population mean differs beyond chance variation under the model assumptions.

Fail to reject H0; all means are exactly equal.

Reject H0; therefore every pair of means is significantly different.





A 100% stacked bar chart shows disease status (yes/no) by exposure group (low/medium/high). The segment proportions differ clearly across exposure groups.
Which statement is best?

This is descriptive evidence variables may not be independent.

This proves causation by exposure.

This shows the two variables must be independent.





You run chisq.test(tab) and see p-value = 0.18.
At alpha = 0.05, which conclusion is best?

Reject H0; there is strong evidence of association.

Fail to reject H0; sample data are compatible with independence (not enough evidence of association).

Accept H0 as true with certainty.








For a one-way ANOVA with 4 groups, which is the correct null?

H0: all four population means are equal.

H0: all four sample means are equal.

H0: at least one population mean differs.





For a chi-square test of independence between treatment arm and side-effect category:

H0: treatment arm and side-effect category are independent in the population.

H0: treatment arm causes side effects.

H0: every cell count in the sample table is equal.





Complete the statement:
“A small p-value means that, if the null were true, data this extreme would be _______.”

likely

uncommon (unlikely)

impossible





Which sentence is best statistical wording when p = 0.003 for an ANOVA overall test?

“There is strong evidence against equal means; such differences would be unlikely by chance alone if all means were equal.”

“The null is impossible and chance never plays a role.”

“This proves treatment mechanism with no assumptions.”








A researcher recruits volunteers from people walking into one gym between 5-6 pm and surveys protein intake.
Can the results be safely generalized to all adults in the city?

Yes, because sample size is all that matters.

Not reliably; this is a convenience sample and may not represent the city population.

Yes, because any sample supports broad generalization.





A simple random sample (SRS) is drawn from a complete city resident list.
Which statement is best?

Generalization to that city population is as safe as we can manage from limited information

No generalization is ever possible, even from SRS.

SRS automatically proves causation.








In an RCT, participants are randomly assigned to drug vs placebo. The drug group has a lower mean symptom score and a hypothesis test comparing the population means has a very small p-value.
Which interpretation is best?

Random assignment helps support a causal interpretation

Because chance exists, random assignment gives no advantage.

RCTs cannot address causation.





In an observational study, soda intake is positively associated with BMI.
Which interpretation is best?

The association could reflect causation, confounding or chance; causation is not established by this design alone.

Observational association proves soda causes higher BMI.

Confounding is impossible in observational studies.





A convenience sample observational study reports a significant association (p < 0.01) between supplement use and fewer colds.
Best interpretation:

Significant p-value alone proves causal effect for everyone.

Evidence of association in the sampled data, but external validity and confounding remain concerns.

Because p is small, sampling design no longer matters.
